industry.name: Handmade artisan bar-soap maker (cold/traditional saponified soap), single-maker, local-pickup only
industry.category: Artisan / handmade physical goods — small local maker (retail product business, not a service trade; not full e-commerce)
industry.notes: Single owner-maker (Jelisha) producing five essential-oil bar soaps from one shared coconut-oil + shea-butter base, sold only by local pickup in Johnson County, Texas. No shipping, no online checkout, no cart. The site is a catalog-plus-trust brochure whose entire job is to route an interested local buyer to a mailto/tel order. Two defining industry traits drive everything: (1) the product is consumable and skin-contact, so ingredient transparency and allergen/lye reassurance are central trust drivers, not nice-to-haves; (2) it is hyper-local pickup-only, so local discoverability and a frictionless contact path matter far more than conversion-funnel polish. Treat this as closer to a farmers'-market maker's table than to a Shopify soap brand.

recommended_pages.name: Home
recommended_pages.reason: Wordmark, tagline, one ingredient/process line, and a 'Shop the Soaps' button. Standard and sufficient as the entry point.

recommended_pages.name: Shop
recommended_pages.reason: The five soaps as a card grid with name, SVG graphic, draft description, and shared pricing. This is the product catalog; for a no-cart maker it doubles as the 'Products' page.

recommended_pages.name: Ingredients & Allergens
recommended_pages.reason: Industry-critical for skin-contact handmade soap. Carries the lye/saponification reassurance and the essential-oil sensitivity note — the two stated customer objections. Keep it.

recommended_pages.name: Contact
recommended_pages.reason: mailto/tel, service-area, and the 3-step ordering sequence. For a pickup-only maker this is the conversion page and must be effortless.

recommended_pages.name: About / maker story
recommended_pages.status: deliberately excluded by client — DO NOT BUILD
recommended_pages.reason: For handmade goods an About/maker-story page is normally a strong trust driver and would usually be recommended. The brief explicitly forbids inventing a personal narrative and says there is NO About page. Flagging the convention only; respect the client and do not build it or smuggle a story into Home.

recommended_pages.name: FAQ
recommended_pages.status: optional / fold-in, not a new page
recommended_pages.reason: The brief lists five real customer questions (lye, ingredients, price, how to order, shipping). These are best answered inline on Ingredients & Allergens, Shop, and Contact rather than as a separate page, keeping the site at the requested four pages. Could also support FAQPage schema.

recommended_features.name: Mobile nav toggle
recommended_features.reason: Most visitors arrive on a phone; a simple accessible hamburger toggle is expected.

recommended_features.name: mailto: and tel: CTAs throughout
recommended_features.reason: These replace add-to-cart everywhere. Repeat them on Home, Shop, and Contact.

recommended_features.name: Click-to-call / click-to-email that pre-fill subject or body
recommended_features.reason: A mailto: link with a prefilled subject like 'Soap order' lowers friction and helps Jelisha triage orders. Phone is a placeholder for now — wire tel: once the real number exists.

recommended_features.name: Pricing stated once, prominently, near top of Shop
recommended_features.reason: Shared flat pricing ($4/bar, 3 for $10) removes the most common purchase hesitation up front.

recommended_features.name: Per-product illustrated SVG with swap-in comment markers
recommended_features.reason: Custom inline SVGs per scent in brand palette; comment each so a real photo drops in later without restructuring.

recommended_features.name: prefers-reduced-motion-respecting hover on product cards only
recommended_features.reason: One subtle hover affordance; no scroll/page-load animation.

standard_ctas: Email or call Jelisha to order (the brief's primary CTA — use verbatim or close)
Email Jelisha to place an order
Have an allergy question? Email before you order
Pick your scents — email or call to reserve
trust_signals: Full, plain-language ingredient disclosure (coconut oil, shea butter, lye/sodium hydroxide, essential oils) — the #1 trust driver for skin-contact handmade soap
Clear, honest lye/saponification explanation: lye reacts completely during saponification and no active lye remains in the cured bar (directly answers the top stated objection)
Up-front allergen transparency: essential oils are a common sensitivity; invitation to ask before ordering
Transparent flat pricing shown without requiring contact ($4/bar, 3 for $10)
Real, named local maker identity (Jelisha) and a real service area (Johnson County, TX) — local + human is the credibility anchor for a maker
Genuine reachable contact: real email now, clearly-marked phone placeholder until the number is added
Small-batch / handmade framing backed by the specific shared base, not vague 'artisan' claims
NOTE: no testimonials, reviews, certifications, awards, or years-in-business were provided — do NOT fabricate any. Honesty and specificity are the trust strategy here.
common_sections: Hero with wordmark + tagline + primary action (Shop / order)
How to order / pickup steps (replaces checkout for a no-cart maker)
Contact block with email, phone, and service area
Short 'how it's made / small-batch' line (one line on Home per brief — NOT a full story section)

copy_tone.formality: Casual but clear and grounded — first-person-friendly, plain-spoken, like a maker talking honestly across a market table. Not corporate, not luxury-boutique, not faux-folksy.
copy_tone.emotion: Warm, calm, reassuring, quietly confident. The dominant job is to put a cautious buyer at ease about ingredients and allergens while keeping the soaps appealing and a little playful (especially 'Eat Me').
keywords_to_avoid: all anti-slop filler and empty superlatives (best-in-class, world-class, top-notch, etc.)
buzzwords (seamless, leverage, elevate, curated luxury experience, etc.)
faux-humble openers ('At Raw Soaps and Suds, we believe...')
'chemical-free' or 'no chemicals' — inaccurate and undercuts the honest lye explanation (soap IS chemistry)
health/medical/therapeutic claims ('detox', 'cures', 'heals', 'antibacterial', 'cleanses toxins') — unsubstantiated and a liability
'lye-free' — false; the correct, trust-building framing is that no ACTIVE lye remains after curing
'100% natural' / 'pure' absolutes not backed by the actual ingredient list
luxury/spa clichés that clash with the rustic, kraft-paper, small-maker identity

competitor_observations: Client listed no competitors, so these are general handmade-soap-maker observations, not data about specific sites.

Typical artisan-soap sites lean heavily on warm cream backgrounds, high-contrast serif type, and terracotta/botanical-green accents — the exact 'AI-default handmade' look the client explicitly wants to avoid. The bluebonnet-blue + kraft direction is a genuine differentiator in this category; lean into it.

Most competitors are full e-commerce (cart, shipping). A pickup-only, contact-to-order maker is rarer online; the opportunity is to make ordering feel personal and frictionless rather than apologizing for the missing cart.
Common competitor weakness: ingredient and lye information is buried or vague. Putting clear, confident ingredient + lye + allergen content front-and-center is an easy credibility win here.
Common competitor weakness: scent descriptions are interchangeable purple prose. Specific, sensory, honest one-liners (and the playful 'Eat Me') will read as more authentic.
Many small-maker sites hide price behind contact. Showing the flat $4 / 3-for-$10 pricing openly removes friction and signals honesty.

recommended_primaries: Client's Bluebonnet blue #3E4C76 (primary) — calm, clean, trustworthy, gentle-on-skin connotations; reads as natural-but-fresh and supports the 'safe/reassuring' goal
Client's Dusty sky blue #8294B8 (secondary) — softens the palette, good for backgrounds/secondary UI
recommended_accents: Client's Kraft paper tan #B8915E — earthy, ties to real soap-packaging materials (kraft/twine/stamp); use sparingly for labels, dividers, the badge, and icon detail as the client specified
colors_to_avoid: Warm cream backgrounds + high-contrast serif + terracotta accents (the AI-default handmade look the client explicitly rejects)
Pure black for body text (client specifies deep indigo-charcoal #2B2F3D instead)
Neon, candy, or synthetic-looking hues that would undercut the 'natural/handmade' message
Heavy botanical/sage-green dominance — would pull the brand back toward the generic artisan-soap norm the client is differentiating away from
color_psychology.reasoning: Blue is an unconventional lead color for handmade soap (the category norm is green/botanical or cream/terracotta), but here that is an asset, not a problem: it is anchored in the Texas bluebonnet, directly supports the stated differentiator, and avoids the AI-default look. Blue also psychologically reads as clean, calm, and trustworthy, which reinforces the central ingredient/lye-reassurance job. The kraft tan accent supplies the warmth and 'handmade materials' texture that blue alone would lack. The palette is internally coherent and well-matched to the brand goals — no concern to flag. Design system is law: these are the client's chosen colors and must not be overridden; this is supportive context only.

layout_conventions.hero_style: Modest, content-first hero — text wordmark + one tagline + a single clear 'Shop the Soaps' button and one short ingredient/process line. No large stock photo (none exist); the optional stamped badge is the one bold visual moment. Mobile-first.
layout_conventions.nav_style: Simple top nav across four pages (Home, Shop, Ingredients & Allergens, Contact) with an accessible mobile hamburger toggle. A sticky header is fine but optional; unlike a trades site, a persistent sticky phone bar is not required — but the email/phone CTA should be easy to reach on every page.
layout_conventions.footer_style: Compact footer repeating email, phone (placeholder), service area (Johnson County, TX — local pickup only), and nav links. No newsletter signup, no social unless provided (none were).
layout_conventions.notes: Structurally this is a small brochure/catalog site: hero → scent grid → ingredient/allergen trust content → ordering steps/contact. The product grid (cards in a responsive grid, NOT numbered 01/02/03) is the structural centerpiece. Repeat the order CTA on Home, Shop, and Contact since there is no cart anywhere. Keep motion minimal (card hover only) and honor prefers-reduced-motion. Accessibility floor per brief: real alt/SVG titles, contrast, visible focus states.

primary_keywords: handmade bar soap near me / local pickup
Cleburne / Burleson handmade soap (nearby Johnson County towns, if accurate)
buy handmade soap local pickup Johnson County
schema_types: LocalBusiness (maker with Johnson County, TX service area; areaServed rather than a published street address)
Product (one per scent — name, description, image/SVG, brand; include price $4 with the 3-for-$10 noted in description)
Offer (optional, attached to Product, with price and availability; note there is NO online purchase — point of sale is contact/pickup, so omit purchase URLs)
FAQPage (for the lye / ingredients / price / how-to-order / shipping questions, if surfaced as Q&A)
